title: About Kaio
content:
# About Me

##### Kaio Barbosa-Chifan

##### (Kai-o bar-Bow-suh key-Fawn)

\
I have an interest in programming as a whole, I get paid for web dev, and I can't seem to shake off the idea of making games for fun.

I was born in California, and grew up in Natal, Brazil, returning to California at age 15. I studied Computer Science at the beautiful campus of UC Santa Cruz.

In the past I have traveled a decent amount, and have lived in a few different countries, but in June 2021 I settled in Seattle where I live with my wife.

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
On November 2nd, I released [https://www.bazaarghost.stream](https://www.bazaarghost.stream) into the wild. The next day, I made [a post on the Bazaar subreddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/PlayTheBazaar/comments/1onkxqn/announcing_bazaarghost_see_your_ghost_go_up/) that went fairly viral, at least in terms of the relative size of the community

Today is November 29th, approaching one month since release, and so far BazaarGhost has seen \~18,000 visitors (with a non-peak average of \~550 visitors per day). Today I'm writing about my experience building the site, reflecting on the experience, and putting down my thoughts about it moving forward.

# What is it?

[BazaarGhost.Stream](http://bazaarghost.stream) is a site that lets you look up matches between streamers and past versions of players' boards, aka *ghosts*.

## About *The Bazaar*

If you know about *The Bazaar* you can skip this section. Otherwise, read on because the site's purpose is not intuitive unless you're familiar with the game.

Here's the basic description of the game:

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
* The Bazaar is an auto-battler with asynchronous PvP
  * You get matched up against other players, but not in realtime
* Combines card game mechanics with the auto-battler genre established by games like Riot Game's *Teamfight Tactics*, or the OG *Dota Auto Chess*
* Gamplay loop: An in-game "day" is comprised of six "hours", where each hour you make decisions between spending gold to buy items, fighting monsters, or other random events leading up to hour six, where you fight the *ghost* of another player—a snapshot of what their board looked like on the same day.
* You start with 20 *prestige.* When you're defeated by another player you lose prestige equal to the day you are on.
* Win as many days as you can (up to 10) before running out of prestige.

Because of *The Bazaar*'s asynchronous nature, a **ghost** version of your board continues on even after you're done playing, and this is where BazaarGhost comes in

## The problem BazaarGhost solves

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
At the moment, there is no in-game way to see how your ghost does against other players from their perspective. A lot of the fun of your game is optimizing your board against unpredictable enemies. Any board you put together, no matter how good, can generally be defeated by something even more powerful for a given matchup. BazaarGhost gives you the satisfaction of seeing your or your friends' ghost go up against streamers of the game on Twitch, along with their reactions to the strength (or weakness!) of the build you managed to cobble together.

# How It Works

Though I have a page about this [on the site](https://www.bazaarghost.stream/how-it-works), in this blog post I want to go into a bit more technical detail.

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
* Find and **catalog** archived streams (VODs) on Twitch that contain at least some gameplay of *The Bazaar*
* **Process** each stream with:
  * **S**treamlink: cli tool to get Twitch `.hls` streams and pipe it into…
  * **F**fmpeg: filters and crops incoming frames to the region of interest, keeping a record of the timestamp associated with each frame.
  * **O**penCV: use `matchTemplate` to determine whether current frame is a matchup screen or not, based on a given template image.
  * **T**esseract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) engine for extracting text from images.
* Save the results to the Postrgres **database** hosted on Supabase
* A very lightweight Next.js **frontend** that searches and surfaces results of username queries, with links to the VOD where the matchup occurred

Whoa the bold letters in that one bullet list spells out **SFOT,** I wonder if that's the lazy shorthand I use to refer to the stream processing step. Yeah, simply put it's a container that takes in a vod id as input and orchestrates S, F, O, and T to spit out usernames with timestamps of where they appear in the VOD.

## How it Started

The first version of BazaarGhost actually started earlier this year, around March 2025. Among the playerbase, I was far from the only one who had the thought of seeing how my ghosts performed vs others.

A search for "see your ghost" in r/PlayTheBazaar over the last 12 months shows the demand for a feature to see how your ghost performs against other players

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
I started with some AI slop-scripts to check if I had any matchups against Kripp, the largest streamer of *The Bazaar.* The prototype was a contrived piece of "works on my machine" ethos, but after seeing it kind of working and letting the concept brew over six months of no-progress while on an intense project at my day job, I got back to work on it towards the end of September/early August.

## This is going to be expensive—unless…?

Yeah, the proof of concept worked, but I was running the SFOT container on my machine. I *could* just update the database once a day, that wasn't the level of "scalable" I wanted. Plus, back-filling thousands of old VODs would've meant letting my PC churn for days, which sounded miserable. The alternative was cloud jobs, I immediately started weighing my options.

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
* Cloud hosted **Virtual Private Server (VPS)** — Services like Hetzner, DigitalOcean, Oracle… a cheap one could be $5 a month, but for what I could get at that price, I was not sure I could process more than once VOD at a time
* **Serverless** — the buzzword of the decade. There was a bit where I felt like I had 12 different `/pricing` tabs from all sorts of services: Google Cloud Jobs, AWS Lambdas, Render, Fly.io, the list goes on. Between trying to calculate my predicted usage, looking for promo and credit offers or differing free trial lengths, I feel like I was on a cheapskate version of hero's journey, trying to outsmart the "creative" pricing strategies of the serverless landscape. Eventually my stubborn frugality paid off when I found…

### The Answer.

Github Actions (GHA). As it turns out, you can run babies all day long as long as you're repo is public and you're not [abusing their infrastructure.](https://docs.github.com/en/site-policy/github-terms/github-terms-for-additional-products-and-features#actions) Since BazaarGhost is neither a product I'm trying to sell nor a state secret, I had no problem building it in the open, and making the best out of the generous—and most importantly—free, github actions.

## Putting it together

With the weight of cost removed, I was giddy to know that my project was feasible at (a relatively small) scale, at little to no cost.

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
<ThemeImage lightSrc="/BazaarGhost/BG-Vod_Processing_Diagram-Light.png" darkSrc="/BazaarGhost/BG-Vod_Processing_Diagram-Dark.png" alt="VOD Processing Diagram" />

### Supabase

Supabase is doing a lot of heavy lifting here. I use [Supabase Edge Functions](https://supabase.com/docs/guides/functions) to handle the discovery side:

* `insert-new-streamers` polls the Twitch API for recent Bazaar VODs and adds any new streamers to the database
* `update-vods` fetches the VOD catalog for each streamer, filtering only for VODs that have Bazaar gameplay chapters

The PostgreSQL database tracks everything: `streamers`, `vods`, `chunks`, and `detections`. A VOD can be hours long, so I split each one into chunks for processing. This is tracked in the `chunks` table, which stores the start/end timestamps and processing status for each segment. When processing completes, the extracted usernames and timestamps land in `detections`.

Supabase Storage holds the frame images for each detection. These actually aren't relevant to the end user, I kept them for debugging purposes. If a username was badly extracted, I could reference the image it rand OCR on to see if there was a mistake with emblem detection, OCR settings, or something else.

Cron jobs handle the scheduling: periodically calling the edge functions to discover new VODs and kicking off the `process-vod` GitHub workflow for any VODs that need processing.

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
The `process-vod` workflow is the bridge between Supabase (control plane) and GitHub Actions (data plane). When a cron job identifies a VOD ready for processing, it triggers `process-vod.yml` via the GitHub API. The workflow first checks if the VOD is still available on Twitch and determines the best quality to use (preferring 480p to balance processing speed and detection accuracy). It then fetches all pending chunks for that VOD from Supabase and spins up parallel jobs to process them.

### SFOT: The Data Plane

The SFOT container is where the actual frame processing happens. It's a Docker image that gets built fresh in each GitHub Actions run (with layer caching to speed things up). The same container runs locally for development.

Here's what the workflow looks like in practice:

```
process-vod.yml triggered for VOD 2345678901
    │
    ├─► check-vod job
    │   └─► Verify VOD available, select quality (480p)
    │
    ├─► fetch-chunks job
    │   └─► Query Supabase for pending chunks → [chunk1, chunk2, ...]
    │
    └─► process-chunk jobs (matrix strategy, up to 20 parallel)
        ├─► chunk1 → SFOT container → detections
        ├─► chunk2 → SFOT container → detections
        ├─► chunk3 → SFOT container → detections
        │   ...
        └─► chunkN → SFOT container → detections
```

title: Bazaar Ghost
content:
Each chunk job builds the SFOT container, then runs it with environment variables pointing to Supabase and the specific chunk ID. The container handles everything from there: fetching chunk details, streaming the VOD segment, extracting frames, detecting matchup screens, running OCR, and uploading results back to Supabase. Failed chunks can be retried independently without reprocessing the entire VOD.

### Frontend

The frontend is intentionally minimal. It's a quick Next.js on Vercel, a search box, and results that link directly to the timestamp in the VOD. I also disabled image optimization, because those use up a ton of Vercel edge function invocations. On the free/hobby tier, it's quite easy to hit the limit. As a consequence, images don't load as fast but since it's not a big part of the UX. Most of the images are the streamer avatars that show up in the search results.

## Final Thoughts

BazaarGhost started as a curiosity, and it is now a great source of fulfillment outside of work, something I was previosuly lacking. It has been so rewarding to see all the dicussion surrounding it, and to see people have positive experiences with the site, even if small.

Thanks for reading, and if you haven't already, go check if your ghost has been spotted at [bazaarghost.stream](https://www.bazaarghost.stream/).

title: Building a Website
content:
I have been creating websites for a long time. In third grade I touched HTML for the first time; there was a week-long after-school program that culminated in writing a page that was supposedly sent to the White House (I'm not really sure why). It's lost to the void of time, but I wish I had some way of seeing it now.

Over the years, developing a website cam to be a marker of my progress. In high-school I knew a photographer that needed a portfolio website. In college, I had some assignments and projects that were on the web. When I needed to get a job? Better update that website.

Throughout it all, there's been a constant tug-and-pull that other developers will be familiar with, and it happens between the two poles of the full DIY method, and the drag-and-drop site builder solution.

Realistically, I want something in-between. I have been trying to find a solution that is highly customizable, but not garbage. And let me tell you, I've got a few piece of garbage sites under my belt. The real answer is, and has always been simplicity! 

Make it **simple**, make it **maintainable**, and make it **frictionless** to add content. The way I have this site set up right now with React, Next.js, and TinaCMS feels right because I feel like I'm addressing all of those points appropriately.

### Simple

title: Building a Website
content:
It is immediately clear from the look of this site that that's what I was aiming for. It's got that minimalist, black-and-white aesthetic that is oh-so-easy to design around. That doesn't mean that I need to be boring! In fact, a lot of the CSS work is pretty straightforward because it's so simple, and I can use that time to make more interesting things like the homepage blob, or the wavy footer. They are small, simple things that serve the purpose of keeping the site looking just the way I like it, and makes it easier for me to express myself.

### Maintainable

Out of the three, this is the one I'm hitting the mark on the least. It's a React site, using Next.js, which let's be honest, will both look extremely different in less than. To keep the architecture of this site future-proof (such that I can throw whatever functionality onto this and have it stick) I will have to put in some elbow-grease, but the trade off for making it easily customizable and quick to get going was worth it for me. Other than those two frameworks, I'm limiting myself from adding a ton of dependencies to the project, besides what's necessary in order to keep my house in order.

title: Building a Website
content:
The good news is that for a static website like this, only a small amount of work is needed to have large positive impacts on longevity. In the grand scheme of things, one can even argue that it's not that important because even if I don't update the architecture of the site at all over a long period of time, it will still stay online, and I'll still be able to add content to it. Regardless, it's still something I think about and it's a good principle to work by. In a previous personal website, I had the experience of cloning its repo, running `npm install` and having absolutely nothing work. I don't ever want that to be the case again!

### Frictionless

This is the most important one for me. It has a lot to do with the title image of this text, and I've fallen victim to the phenomenon of spending countless hours making a custom website only to never touch it again. The links break, pages disappear, and obsolete information takes the spotlight for all of eternity to see.

To address this, I google'd around and fortunately came across Tina, a headless CMS that I can pop into my site, log into it from any machine, and make updates to it that are integrated with git. I can also customize it for the type of data I want to display, but that is for another article and another time. 

Did I publish something and notice a typo? I can fix that in under a minute.

## What is This Site, Anyway?

title: Building a Website
content:
There's no format. That's the point of having this thing in the first place. I do feel that is is good for both my personal and professional development to have something public facing, and social media exists! The only problem is that I barely post on Twitter, my Facebook has gathered cobwebs years ago, and Instagram is a distant memory. Rather than try to get myself to do something I do not naturally  gravitate towards (using social media) I'd rather try this.

It takes longer, but it's more fun to have a site I built. Why not build something I have full creative control over? A place where I can ramble, keep a record of milestones in my life, and write something I think someone else might find useful.

Sure, it's not perfect. I'm sure I'll be creating more problems of my own by coding the front-end myself, but that's kind of the point. I'll have something to tinker on, improve over time, and genuinely call my own.

title: Understanding L Systems
content:
This week, I made a point to dive into something I've held an interest in for a long time, prodded on by the rediscovery of the book "[The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants](algorithmicbotany.org/papers/abop/abop.pdf)" whose mere title made such an impact that I have not been able to forget about it. It is a textbook all about the fascinating world of modeling plants algorithmically. 

I could go on about all the things I want to do related to the topic of the textbook, but before I go on too long of a tangent, I'm going to stop myself short, and discuss (mostly for my own sake) the first framework needed to understand algorithmic plant modeling: L Systems.

# L Systems?

Named after Aristid Lindenmayer, Lindenmayer Systems were first created with the intention of representing the development of cellular plant life and blah blah boring and certainly very important history.

The point is, I've known *about* them for a long time for the simple fact that "L Systems" is a term that comes up a lot alongside fractals, and just like most people that have even a marginal interest in math, programming, or just nature in general, I have found fractals as fascinating as they are elusive.

They main idea is pretty simple, but sounds complex at first. Before I say anything about it, just know that the L Systems create pretty shapes by repeating other shapes as much as you want, forever.

Got it? Okay, so you have a \*grammar, \*a *generator* and a *producer.*

title: Understanding L Systems
content:
Using the grammar, I can define a generator that spawns producers. Producers create more producers.

This is accomplished by giving a turtle simple but specific instructions.

# Yes, a Turtle.

A helpful way to visualize L-Systems is by imagining a turtle that understands only the grammar defined by the L-System (this is not some cute metaphor I came up with, the turtle idea is in the textbook and has existed for a long time).

The turtle moves based on specific symbols, such as:

F: move forward
\+: turn clockwise
\-: turn counter-clockwise

# Generators and Producers

In an L-System, the generator serves as the initial shape or structure from which the pattern will be developed. The generator is a string composed of symbols, which are defined by the grammar. The producer, on the other hand, is responsible for interpreting the grammar and creating new shapes or structures based on the rules provided.

As the L-System iterates, the producer continually rewrites the generator string, replacing certain symbols with new ones according to the grammar rules. The process of rewriting allows the pattern to grow and evolve, generating intricate and complex patterns over time.

# Starting to Code

To gain a better understanding of L-Systems, I created a simple program in C using the SDL2 library. The program sets up an SDL environment and displays a window with the resulting L-System pattern.

title: Understanding L Systems
Understanding L Systems
BazaarGhost - Community Reception and Growth
content: Kaio launched BazaarGhost on November 2, 2025 and announced it the next day on r/PlayTheBazaar. The post went viral relative to the community size. Within the first month the site attracted approximately 18,000 unique visitors with a sustained average of around 550 daily visitors. The project addressed a widely-felt need: there was no in-game way to see how your ghost performed against other players, and multiple Reddit threads had requested this feature. BazaarGhost gave players the satisfaction of seeing their builds matched against known streamers like Kripp, complete with reactions. The project originated as a personal curiosity with prototype scripts to check matchups against Kripp, then matured over six months into a full system with a web app, Discord bot, and automated processing pipeline. Kaio has described it as a major source of fulfillment outside of his day job.
metadata: {"url": "https://www.bazaarghost.stream", "repo": "kaiobarb/bazaar-ghost", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": [], "status": "active", "highlights": ["18,000 visitors in first month", "~550 daily visitors sustained", "Viral Reddit launch", "Addressed widely-requested community feature", "Grew from prototype to full system over 6 months"]}

title: BazaarGhost - Frontend and Discord Bot
content: The BazaarGhost frontend (bazaarghost.stream) is a Next.js application deployed on Vercel with ISR (1-hour revalidation). The main feature is a fuzzy username search (via a Supabase RPC function with similarity threshold) with a streamer dropdown filter, paginated results, and URL-based navigation for shareable search links. Detection results show an embedded Twitch player that auto-seeks to the exact matchup timestamp, rank emblem display, confidence scores, and truncated username warnings. The landing page shows top streamers with recent detections and global stats. Content pages (how-it-works, contact, donate) use MDX. Kaio disabled Next.js image optimization to stay within Vercel free-tier limits. In addition to the web frontend, Kaio built a Discord bot as a Supabase edge function with slash commands: /search, /notify (subscribe to username detections), /list, /setchannel, /clearchannel, and /help. The bot sends DMs or server channel messages when a subscribed username is detected.
metadata: {"url": "https://www.bazaarghost.stream", "repo": "kaiobarb/bazaarghost.stream", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": ["Next.js", "TypeScript", "React", "Vercel", "Supabase", "MDX", "shadcn/ui", "Discord API", "Twitch Player API"], "status": "active", "highlights": ["Fuzzy search with similarity threshold", "Embedded Twitch player with auto-seek", "Discord bot with notification subscriptions", "ISR for performance", "Free-tier optimized"]}

title: BazaarGhost - Zero-Cost Infrastructure
content: Kaio designed BazaarGhost infrastructure around a zero-cost constraint. After evaluating VPS providers and serverless platforms, he chose GitHub Actions as free compute since the repo is public and non-commercial. Supabase serves as the control plane: edge functions poll the Twitch API (using the unofficial GraphQL endpoint for VOD chapter data not available in the Helix API) to discover new streamers and catalog VODs with Bazaar gameplay segments. Cron jobs trigger the process-vod GitHub Actions workflow for pending VODs. The workflow uses a matrix strategy to spin up to 20 parallel chunk-processing jobs per VOD, each running the Dockerized SFDE container with BuildKit caching. Twitch EventSub webhooks provide near-real-time indexing: when a tracked streamer goes offline, Twitch sends a webhook to a Supabase edge function that automatically fetches the new VOD and triggers processing. PostgreSQL on Supabase tracks streamers, VODs, chunks, detections, and notification subscriptions. The architecture separates the control plane (Supabase) from the data plane (GitHub Actions).
metadata: {"url": "https://www.bazaarghost.stream", "repo": "kaiobarb/bazaar-ghost", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": ["GitHub Actions", "Supabase", "PostgreSQL", "Supabase Edge Functions", "Docker", "Twitch Helix API", "Twitch GraphQL API", "Twitch EventSub"], "status": "active", "highlights": ["Zero hosting cost", "Control plane / data plane separation", "Up to 20 parallel jobs per VOD", "Near-real-time via EventSub webhooks", "Unofficial Twitch GraphQL for chapter data"]}

title: BazaarGhost - Overview
content: BazaarGhost (bazaarghost.stream) is a tool that lets players of The Bazaar, an asynchronous auto-battler game, look up how their ghost (a snapshot of their board) performed against Twitch streamers. It works by indexing archived Twitch VODs, detecting matchup screens via computer vision, and extracting player usernames via OCR. Kaio identified the idea from community demand on r/PlayTheBazaar, prototyped it in March 2025, and launched publicly on November 2, 2025. The project spans two open-source repos: kaiobarb/bazaar-ghost (Python CV pipeline, Supabase edge functions, GitHub Actions workflows, database migrations, Discord bot) and kaiobarb/bazaarghost.stream (Next.js frontend). Kaio is the sole developer with 120+ commits across both repos and 70+ closed PRs on the backend alone.
metadata: {"url": "https://www.bazaarghost.stream", "repo": "kaiobarb/bazaar-ghost", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": ["Python", "TypeScript", "Next.js", "Supabase", "PostgreSQL", "GitHub Actions", "Docker", "OpenCV", "PaddleOCR", "FFmpeg", "Streamlink", "Discord API", "OpenTelemetry"], "status": "active", "highlights": ["120+ commits across two repos", "70+ closed PRs on backend", "Fully open source", "End-to-end: CV pipeline, infrastructure, frontend, Discord bot"]}

title: BazaarGhost - SFDE Processing Pipeline
content: The core of BazaarGhost is SFDE (Stream, Filter, Detect, Extract), a Dockerized Python pipeline that extracts player matchup data from Twitch VODs. It orchestrates 4 parallel threads connected by queues. Streamlink downloads VOD segments via HLS at 480p. FFmpeg applies a crop region from a streamer-specific SFDE profile and extracts JPEG frames at 0.5 FPS (one frame every 2 seconds, sufficient because matchup screens last 5-6 seconds). OpenCV performs emblem detection via template matching against rank badge images (bronze through legend) at 4 resolutions with alpha-channel masks, then locates the right boundary of the opponent nameplate to handle camera overlays that partially occlude it. PaddleOCR extracts the opponent username from the preprocessed region after emblem masking. The OCR engine evolved from Tesseract to EasyOCR to PaddleOCR (PP-OCRv5 mobile models) through experimentation on dedicated branches. VODs are split into 30-minute chunks processed independently, enabling parallel execution and per-chunk retries.
metadata: {"url": "https://www.bazaarghost.stream", "repo": "kaiobarb/bazaar-ghost", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": ["Python", "Docker", "OpenCV", "PaddleOCR", "FFmpeg", "Streamlink"], "status": "active", "highlights": ["4 parallel threads connected by queues", "Per-streamer SFDE profiles for custom overlays", "Multi-resolution template matching with alpha masks", "OCR engine evolved through 3 engines via branch experimentation", "Emblem masking before OCR for cleaner extraction"]}

title: BazaarGhost - Observability and Testing
content: BazaarGhost has a comprehensive observability and testing setup. The SFDE pipeline emits OpenTelemetry spans, counters, and histograms, while edge functions push structured logs to Grafana Loki and metrics to Prometheus via OTLP. A custom JSON logger provides structured logging with processing metrics and health status throughout the pipeline. The test suite uses 170+ annotated fixture frames with accuracy thresholds: 98% for emblem detection and 90% for clean-frame OCR extraction. Tests are organized per quality level and per rank, with a custom pytest report plugin that generates GitHub step summaries. The CI workflow (test-sfde.yml) builds the Docker image and runs the full test suite on every push or PR to main/dev that touches the sfde/ directory. Kaio also added confidence-score histograms to tune detection accuracy over time and tracks telemetry across environments.
metadata: {"url": "https://www.bazaarghost.stream", "repo": "kaiobarb/bazaar-ghost", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": ["OpenTelemetry", "Grafana Cloud", "Grafana Loki", "Prometheus", "pytest", "GitHub Actions CI"], "status": "active", "highlights": ["170+ annotated test fixtures", "98% emblem detection accuracy threshold", "90% OCR accuracy threshold", "Custom pytest report plugin for GitHub summaries", "Full OTLP observability to Grafana Cloud"]}

title: hnswlib-wasm - API Surface and Test Coverage
content: The hnswlib-wasm API mirrors hnswlib-node exactly, with the only difference being an async WASM module initialization step. It exposes five classes: HierarchicalNSW (the main HNSW approximate nearest-neighbor index with methods for initIndex, addPoint, searchKnn, markDelete, resizeIndex, read/write index, and configurable ef parameter), BruteforceSearch (exact nearest-neighbor), L2Space, InnerProductSpace, and cosine distance (handled by normalizing vectors and using inner product). All three distance metrics (L2, inner product, cosine) are supported. The searchKnn method supports optional filter functions for constrained search. The test suite covers all five classes with Jest, including error cases (wrong argument types, uninitialized index, dimension mismatches) and correctness of distance computations across all metrics.
metadata: {"url": "https://www.npmjs.com/package/hnsw-wasm", "repo": "kaiobarb/hnswlib-wasm", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": ["C++", "WebAssembly", "TypeScript", "Jest"], "status": "archived", "highlights": ["API mirrors hnswlib-node exactly", "Supports L2, inner product, and cosine distance", "Filter functions for constrained search", "Comprehensive Jest test suite"]}

title: hnswlib-wasm - WASM Bindings for Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search
content: Kaio built hnswlib-wasm, a WebAssembly binding for the hnswlib C++ library (a well-known approximate nearest-neighbor search library with 4,000+ stars). The project brings high-performance vector similarity search to browser and non-Node WASM environments. The key technical insight was using emnapi (a Node-API implementation for Emscripten) to reuse the existing N-API C++ bindings from hnswlib-node rather than writing WASM bindings from scratch. This approach let Kaio wrap the full hnswlib API surface (HNSW index, brute-force search, L2/inner-product/cosine distance spaces) in a single ~1,300-line C++ file compiled via Emscripten to WASM. The build supports three WASM targets: Emscripten (primary, with pthreads and a pool of 4 threads for multi-threaded index operations), WASI threads, and bare wasm32. Published on npm as hnsw-wasm with full TypeScript definitions. The project has 6 GitHub stars.
metadata: {"url": "https://www.npmjs.com/package/hnsw-wasm", "repo": "kaiobarb/hnswlib-wasm", "role": "Solo developer", "stack": ["C++", "WebAssembly", "Emscripten", "emnapi", "CMake", "TypeScript", "JavaScript", "Node-API"], "status": "archived", "highlights": ["Reused hnswlib-node N-API bindings via emnapi bridge", "Multi-threaded WASM with pthreads pool", "3 WASM targets: Emscripten, WASI threads, bare wasm32", "6 GitHub stars", "Full TypeScript definitions"]}

title: ShaderWeb - AI-Powered Practice and Validation
content: ShaderWeb uses Claude Sonnet 4 via the Vercel AI SDK for two core features. Practice iteration generation streams structured JSON (via streamText with Output.object and Zod schema validation) to create new variations of exercises that test the same concept from different creative angles. Users can provide custom directions like use warm colors or make it about space. The client uses experimental_useObject from @ai-sdk/react for progressive streaming with skeleton placeholders. Code validation uses Claude at lower temperature (0.3) to evaluate conceptual correctness rather than exact code matching, returning pass/fail, feedback, and lists of concepts understood vs. missed. On pass, module progress is automatically updated. Iteration history persists to Supabase, allowing users to revisit and continue from any past iteration. The Express backend handles both endpoints (/api/iterate and /api/validate) with careful prompt engineering that includes the module goals, content, and reference code as context.

title: ShaderWeb - Interactive Knowledge Graph with WebGL Heatmap
content: ShaderWeb features an interactive knowledge graph built with d3-force simulation rendered as SVG. Four node types are visually distinct: concepts (circles, indigo), modules (status-colored circles), capstones (diamonds, yellow), and artifacts (squares, pink). Three edge types (depends-on, teaches, relates-to) display contextual directional arrows on hover. The graph supports pan, zoom (cursor-centric mouse wheel), and node dragging. Behind the SVG graph, a real WebGL shader renders a gaussian heatmap where each node's heat is weighted by how many other modules it unlocks (depends-on edge count). The heatmap uses a cold-to-hot color ramp and subtly pulses with a time-based sine wave. Node positions from d3-force are passed to the shader via a float texture, and pan/zoom uniforms are synchronized between the SVG overlay and the WebGL canvas.

title: ShaderWeb - Personalized GLSL Learning Platform
content: ShaderWeb is a personalized GLSL shader learning platform Kaio is building. It combines a graph-based curriculum of 200+ modules (drawn from The Book of Shaders, the OpenGL Shading Language Orange Book, and the OpenGL 4 Shading Language Cookbook) with a live GLSL editor, real-time WebGL preview, and AI-powered practice generation. The curriculum is structured as a knowledge graph rather than a linear path: modules are atomic building blocks, concepts are thematic groupings, and capstones synthesize multiple modules. The platform covers GLSL fundamentals, shaping functions, procedural textures, lighting models, generative noise, post-processing, and more. Users edit GLSL in a CodeMirror 6 editor with instant visual feedback via a WebGL canvas, generate practice variations using Claude Sonnet 4, and validate solutions against conceptual correctness (not exact code matching). Built with React 19, Vite, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, an Express backend, and Supabase for persistence.
